preamble.summary: A psalm of trust amid pursuit, attributed to David when the Philistines seized him in Gath. David confesses his fear, declares his trust in God's word, and expresses confidence that God keeps a record of his tears. The psalm features a refrain — 'In God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can flesh do to me?' — that punctuates the movement from terror to faith.
preamble.remarkable: Verse 9 (Hebrew) contains one of the most intimate images of God in the entire Bible: 'Put my tears in your bottle; are they not in your record?' David imagines God carefully collecting each tear — not wiping them away, not stopping them, but preserving them as evidence that matters. The no'd ('bottle, wineskin') was a traveler's essential possession; David pictures God as carrying David's grief with him. The metaphor says: your suffering is not wasted, not forgotten, not invisible. God keeps receipts. This is not stoic theology — it is a God who is moved by human pain and stores it as something precious.
preamble.friction: The superscription references David's capture by the Philistines in Gath (1 Samuel 21:10-15), where David feigned madness to escape. But Psalm 34's superscription covers the same event using the name 'Abimelech' instead of 'Achish.' These may reflect different traditions about the same event, or the name 'Abimelech' in Psalm 34 may be a dynastic title rather than a personal name. The phrase yonat elem rechoqim in the superscription (v. 1) is obscure — possibly a melody name meaning 'the silent dove of distant places' or 'the dove of the distant terebinths.'
preamble.connections: The Gath episode is in 1 Samuel 21:10-15. The refrain 'what can flesh do to me?' is quoted in Hebrews 13:6. The tear-bottle image connects to Revelation 21:4 ('God will wipe every tear from their eyes'). David's vow-language in the closing verses connects to the thank-offering tradition of Leviticus 7 and Psalm 50:14.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֤חַ ׀ עַל־יוֹנַ֖ת אֵ֥לֶם רְחֹקִ֗ים לְדָוִ֥ד מִכְתָּ֑ם בֶּאֱחֹ֨ז אוֹת֖וֹ פְלִשְׁתִּ֣ים בְּגַֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Jonathelemrechokim, Michtam of David, when the Philistines took him in Gath.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. According to "The Silent Dove of Distant Places."
A miktam of David, when the Philistines seized him in Gath.
translator_notes: yonat elem rechoqim is one of the most mysterious phrases in the psalm superscriptions. yonah ('dove') connects to David's dove-wish in Psalm 55:7; elem ('silence, muteness') may describe the dove or David's own silenced state; rechoqim ('distant ones, far-off places') captures David's situation as a fugitive far from home. If this is a melody name, it evokes the theme of vulnerable exile. miktam appears in six psalm titles (Psalms 16, 56-60) and its meaning is uncertain — possibly from katam ('to cover, to conceal') suggesting a hidden or precious song, or from ketem ('gold'), suggesting something golden or valuable.

verses.text_hebrew: חׇנֵּ֣נִי אֱ֭לֹהִים כִּֽי־שְׁאָפַ֣נִי אֱנ֑וֹשׁ כׇּל־הַ֝יּ֗וֹם לֹחֵ֥ם יִלְחָצֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Be merciful unto me, O God: for man would swallow me up; he fighting daily oppresseth me.
verses.rendering:
Be gracious to me, God, for people trample me;
all day long an attacker presses me.
translator_notes: she'afani ('trample me, crush me, pant after me') can mean either to snap at (like a beast) or to trample underfoot. enosh ('mortal, human being') is used rather than ish ('man'), emphasizing the frailty of the oppressor — merely human. The irony: a mortal is trampling another mortal, as if one had authority over the other.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁאֲפ֣וּ שׁ֭וֹרְרַי כׇּל־הַיּ֑וֹם כִּֽי־רַ֝בִּ֗ים לֹחֲמִ֥ים לִ֗י מָרֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Mine enemies would daily swallow me up: for they be many that fight against me, O thou most High.
verses.rendering:
My enemies trample me all day long,
for many are fighting against me, O Most High.
translator_notes: The repetition of kol ha-yom ('all day long') from verse 2 emphasizes the relentless nature of the persecution. marom ('Most High, the height') — David addresses God by a title that places God above all the enemies who surround him below. The contrast is spatial: David is pressed on the ground level, but God is above it all.

verses.text_kjv: What time I am afraid, I will trust in thee.
verses.rendering:
On the day I am afraid,
I will trust in you.
translator_notes: The brevity of the Hebrew is striking — six words in Hebrew, no embellishment, no theological argument. The simplicity itself is the theology: when afraid, trust. The relationship between fear and trust is not sequential (first stop being afraid, then trust) but simultaneous (afraid and trusting at the same time).

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּאלֹהִ֗ים אֲהַלֵּ֥ל דְּבָר֑וֹ בֵּאלֹהִ֣ים בָּ֭טַחְתִּי לֹ֣א אִירָ֑א מַה־יַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה בָשָׂ֣ר לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: In God I will praise his word, in God I have put my trust; I will not be afraid what flesh can do unto me.
verses.rendering:
In God — I praise his word.
In God I trust; I will not be afraid.
What can flesh do to me?
verses.expanded_rendering: The first occurrence of the refrain. David praises God's davar ('word') — not a specific promise but God's entire spoken reality, everything God has declared about himself and his commitment to his people. Then the trust declaration: be-Elohim batachti ('in God I trust') — the perfect tense indicates completed, settled trust, not a future intention. The consequence flows: lo ira ('I will not be afraid'). And the rhetorical question that demolishes fear: mah ya'aseh basar li ('what can flesh do to me?'). basar ('flesh') is the Hebrew word for human weakness, mortality, the temporary nature of all living things. David's enemies are flesh — temporary, fragile, destined to end. God is not flesh. The question answers itself.
translator_notes: The refrain appears here (v. 5) and again in verses 11-12, framing the psalm's center. Hebrews 13:6 echoes this refrain: 'The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can anyone do to me?' The move from 'flesh' (basar) to 'anyone' (anthropos) in the Greek translation preserves the point: human opposition is finite.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הַ֭יּוֹם דְּבָרַ֣י יְעַצֵּ֑בוּ עָלַ֖י כׇּל־מַחְשְׁבֹתָ֣ם לָרָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Every day they wrest my words: all their thoughts are against me for evil.
verses.rendering:
All day long they twist my words;
all their thoughts are against me for evil.
translator_notes: ye'atsevu devarai ('they twist/distort my words') — the verb atsav can mean 'to grieve, to hurt, to twist, to shape.' David's words are being reshaped by his enemies — taken out of context, reinterpreted, weaponized. The information war precedes the physical one.

verses.text_hebrew: יָג֤וּרוּ ׀ יִצְפּ֗וֹנוּ הֵ֭מָּה עֲקֵבַ֣י יִשְׁמֹ֑רוּ כַּ֝אֲשֶׁ֗ר קִוּ֥וּ נַפְשִֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: They gather themselves together, they hide themselves, they mark my steps, when they wait for my soul.
verses.rendering:
They band together, they lurk,
they watch my steps,
as they wait for my life.
translator_notes: Three verbs of surveillance: yaguru ('they band together, they attack'), yitsponu ('they hide, they lurk'), and yishmoru ('they watch, they keep guard over'). The enemies are organized, hidden, and patient. aqevai ('my steps, my heels') — they track David's movements. The hunting imagery is precise: a coordinated team stalking prey.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־אָ֥וֶן פַּלֶּט־לָ֑מוֹ בְּ֝אַ֗ף עַמִּ֤ים ׀ הוֹרֵ֬ד אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Shall they escape by iniquity? in thine anger cast down the people, O God.
verses.rendering:
For their wickedness — will they escape?
In anger, God, bring down the peoples!
translator_notes: The rhetorical question expects a negative answer: no, they will not escape on account of (al) their wickedness. David then asks God to act: be-af ('in anger') hored ('bring down, cast down') ammim ('peoples, nations'). The plural 'peoples' may refer to the Philistines or to all hostile nations. God's anger is not arbitrary rage but the appropriate response of justice to sustained wickedness.

verses.text_hebrew: נֹדִ֗י סָפַ֥רְתָּה אָ֑תָּה שִׂ֥ימָה דִ֝מְעָתִ֗י בְנֹאדֶ֥ךָ הֲלֹ֣א בְּסִפְרָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou tellest my wanderings: put thou my tears into thy bottle: are they not in thy book?
verses.rendering:
You have counted my wanderings.
Put my tears in your bottle —
are they not in your record?
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is a masterpiece of intimacy between the suffering human and the attentive God. nodi sapartah attah ('my wanderings you have counted — you!') — the emphatic attah ('you') insists on God's personal attention. nod means 'wandering, homelessness, exile' — David's fugitive life. God has not merely observed it; he has sapar ('counted, recorded') every step. Then the astonishing request: simah dim'ati be-no'dekha ('put my tears in your bottle'). The no'd is a leather water-skin or wineskin — a traveler's most essential possession. David imagines God carrying a skin not of water but of tears. Each tear is collected, preserved, kept. The final question: halo be-sifratekha ('are they not in your record/accounting?') — David believes God maintains a ledger of human suffering. Nothing is lost. Nothing is overlooked. The God of the universe keeps a bottle of David's tears.
translator_notes: The wordplay between nodi ('my wandering') and no'dekha ('your bottle/skin') connects David's homeless journeying with God's careful preservation. The wandering produces tears; the tears are stored. There is a poetic justice: what exile costs David, God saves.

sifrathekha ('your record, your account') is from the same root as sefer ('book, scroll'). God is depicted as keeping written records — the same image appears in Malachi 3:16 ('a scroll of remembrance') and Daniel 7:10 ('the books were opened').

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֗ז יָ֭שׁוּבוּ אוֹיְבַ֣י אָח֑וֹר בְּי֥וֹם אֶ֝קְרָ֗א זֶה־יָ֘דַ֤עְתִּי כִּֽי־אֱלֹהִ֣ים לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: When I cry unto thee, then shall mine enemies turn back: this I know; for God is for me.
verses.rendering:
Then my enemies will turn back
on the day I call.
This I know: God is for me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The logic is simple and absolute: when David calls (be-yom eqra, 'on the day I call'), his enemies will retreat (yashuvu achor, 'turn back, backward'). The confidence rests on a single piece of knowledge: zeh yada'ti ki Elohim li ('this I know — that God is for me'). The word li ('for me, on my side, belonging to me') is the psalm's theological foundation. God is not neutral. God is not absent. God is li — for David, on David's side, aligned with David against those who hunt him. Paul will echo this in Romans 8:31: 'If God is for us, who can be against us?'
translator_notes: zeh yada'ti ('this I know') is emphatic — it is not hope or wish but settled knowledge. The basis of the knowledge is not David's worthiness but God's character. God is 'for' the vulnerable one who trusts, not 'for' the powerful one who oppresses.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֭אלֹהִים אֲהַלֵּ֣ל דָּבָ֑ר בַּ֝יהֹוָ֗ה אֲהַלֵּ֥ל דָּבָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: In God will I praise his word: in the LORD will I praise his word.
verses.rendering:
In God — I praise his word.
In the LORD — I praise his word.
translator_notes: The refrain now doubles the divine name: Elohim and YHWH. The expansion from one name to two intensifies the praise — David invokes God both as the universal Creator (Elohim) and as the covenant LORD (YHWH). Both identities are praised for the same thing: his davar ('word').

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּאלֹהִ֣ים בָּ֭טַחְתִּי לֹ֣א אִירָ֑א מַה־יַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה אָדָ֣ם לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: In God have I put my trust: I will not be afraid what man can do unto me.
verses.rendering:
In God I trust; I will not be afraid.
What can a mere human do to me?
translator_notes: The refrain repeats from verse 5 with one change: basar ('flesh') is replaced by adam ('human being, mortal'). The substitution reinforces the point from a different angle — basar emphasizes physical frailty; adam emphasizes creaturely status. Whether viewed as fragile flesh or as a creature under God, the enemy is no match for the God David trusts.

verses.text_hebrew: עָלַ֣י אֱ֭לֹהִים נְדָרֶ֑יךָ אֲשַׁלֵּ֖ם תּוֹדֹ֣ת לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy vows are upon me, O God: I will render praises unto thee.
verses.rendering:
Upon me, God, are my vows to you;
I will fulfill thank offerings to you.
translator_notes: nedarekha ('your vows' or 'my vows to you') — David acknowledges that he has made promises to God during his distress and now commits to fulfilling them. todot ('thank offerings, praises') — from the root yadah ('to give thanks, to confess'). The todah is both a sacrifice and a declaration of gratitude.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י הִצַּ֪לְתָּ נַפְשִׁ֡י מִמָּ֗וֶת הֲלֹ֣א רַ֭גְלַי מִדֶּ֑חִי לְהִתְהַלֵּ֥ךְ לִ֝פְנֵ֗י אֱלֹהִ֥ים בְּא֣וֹר הַחַיִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou hast delivered my soul from death: wilt not thou deliver my feet from falling, that I may walk before God in the light of the living?
verses.rendering:
For you have delivered my life from death,
my feet from stumbling,
so that I may walk before God
in the light of the living.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes with the widest possible horizon. God has done two things: delivered David's nefesh ('life') from death and his raglai ('feet') from dechi ('stumbling, being pushed down'). The purpose (lehithalekh lifnei Elohim) is not merely survival but walking before God — living in God's presence with awareness and integrity. The destination is be-or ha-chayyim ('in the light of the living') — a phrase that means life itself as illumination, the experience of being alive in God's world as a kind of radiance. David began the psalm seized by Philistines in darkness and fear. He ends it walking freely in light.
translator_notes: or ha-chayyim ('the light of the living') contrasts with the darkness of Sheol and death that threatened throughout the psalm. Light in Hebrew poetry is nearly synonymous with life, blessing, and God's presence (Psalm 36:9: 'In your light we see light'). David's rescue is not just from death but into a quality of life defined by God's luminous presence.
